First, I apologise for the direct approach in raising what should be an everyday complaint to such a high executive level. That said, I have not had any success in addressing this concern through traditional channels, and I am hopeful that one or both of you can assist me in finding a resolution.

My girlfriend and I stayed at the Sahara Las Vegas from February 26-28, 2026. While we loved the property overall, our stay was significantly affected by a large oversight on the part of hotel staff.

On the night of the 26th, when settling into sleep, I discovered that my room (516) did not have an electrical outlet anywhere within reach of the bed. I use a CPAP machine to treat my diagnosed sleep apnea — unfortunately, sleeping without the machine constitutes a significant health risk to me, and can and has led to health complications in the past.

In light of this, I promptly called the guest services desk to request to borrow an extension cord. This simple solution would have allowed me to continue to use the room safely and effectively. At first, I was informed that hotel policy forbade lending out items such as this. Instead, the guest services operator suggested I look at the in-house gift shop to purchase one.

When I mentioned my sleep apnea, and that this was a request for disability accommodation, the operator said, “oh, hold on” and, without further explanation, placed the call on hold. After fifteen minutes of waiting with no reply, the call disconnected. I called the front desk again — this time from my personal phone instead of the room phone — and had similar results.

At this stage, I did call the in house gift shop, who said they do not stock extension cords.

That night, I attempted to sleep without the CPAP, unfortunately resulting in a poor night’s sleep for both myself and my girlfriend, and significant physical discomfort and health impacts the following day.

The next night, I repeated my effort to call the front desk, and, on failing this, went downstairs to speak to the hotel reception. My experience on the phone was the same — first refusal, then an unexplained hold when I cited a medical necessity. My experience at the reception was no more fruitful, with the person I spoke with suggesting I return at an unspecified later time to speak with a supervisor.

That night, I slept in a pile of blankets and pillows on the hotel floor, so my CPAP machine could reach the wall. This had the unfortunate knock-on effect of blocking my girlfriend’s nighttime access to the bathroom, which is accessible only through a sliding door recessed into the wall — which, if closed, would block the outlet I was using.

I’m an experienced traveller and a generally pretty low maintenance person, and I don’t make a habit of writing complaint letters of this nature. However, in this case, I have to make an exception. Visiting from Montreal, I was led to believe that Las Vegas, and the United States more generally, have strong and robust protections in place, such as the ADA, to protect those with disabilities. My experience at the Sahara has led me to question that understanding — in particular, the unresponsiveness and poor communication I experienced from your staff regarding this issue, even after I expressed that it was related to an urgent medical problem.

I am confident that this experience is an outlying one and does not reflect the values of your resort — which, in many other aspects, was a lovely place I would be happy to return to. I am hopeful that one or both of you can work with me to find a resolution to this issue that reflects the severity of the neglect, the health and disability risks posed, and the loss of enjoyment of an otherwise memorable vacation.

Thanks in advance for your time and consideration, and for taking the time to read an admittedly long story.

Best regards,
Jonathan Haines
514-913-6086
